text: Hi, this is a patient that had a Roux-en-Y bypass approximately a month ago at a nearby hospital.
She presented to the emergency department with severe abdominal pain.
She had an x-ray, showed free air under the diaphragm, CAT scan that showed Perforation possible at the JJ anastomosis with no evidence of obstruction.
The surgical view shows the conduit appearing normal in size, with a notable presence of air adjacent to the JJ anastomosis.
So after resuscitation, took her to the OR and decided to explore, did the diagnostic laparoscopy, adopted robots, she tolerated pneumo well.
The surgeon carefully removes the greater omentum to expose and visualize the JJ anastomosis site.

So here, like I said, just decided to kind of repair it. We did ICG, blood flow was pretty good. Repaired it.
The surgeon places an omental patch over the repaired area, ensuring coverage and protection of the site.
The surgeon inspects the mucosa, which appears healthy and intact inside the surgical site.
The surgeon attempts to elevate the gastric remnant but encounters tension. To alleviate this, they release the attachment of the greater omentum to the stomach by carefully opening it.
A 20 French balloon G-tube is inserted and positioned, with the stomach being approximated to the posterior aspect of the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon performs a laparoscopic washout of the abdominal cavity, placing multiple drains in the right upper quadrant, left upper quadrant, and pelvis.
